exercises.title: Returning a Single Value
exercises.description: return sends a value back to the caller. The caller can use that value directly.
exercises.starterCode:
def celsius_to_kelvin(celsius: float) -> float:
    """Convert Celsius to Kelvin."""
    return celsius + 273.15

def fahrenheit_to_celsius(fahrenheit: float) -> float:
    """Convert Fahrenheit to Celsius."""
    return (fahrenheit - 32) * 5 / 9

# TODO: Convert 100°C to Kelvin and print it
kelvin = celsius_to_kelvin(100)
print(f"100°C = {kelvin}K")

# TODO: Convert 212°F to Celsius and print it
celsius = fahrenheit_to_celsius(212)
print(f"212°F = {celsius:.1f}°C")

# TODO: Chain the functions: convert 0°F to Celsius, then to Kelvin
kelvin2 = celsius_to_kelvin(fahrenheit_to_celsius(0))
print(f"0°F = {kelvin2:.2f}K")
hints: Store the return value in a variable: kelvin = celsius_to_kelvin(100)
Or use the return value directly: celsius_to_kelvin(fahrenheit_to_celsius(0))

exercises.title: Returning Multiple Values
exercises.description: Python functions can return multiple values as a tuple. Unpack them when calling.
exercises.starterCode:
def min_max(numbers: list) -> tuple:
    """Return the minimum and maximum of a list."""
    return min(numbers), max(numbers)

def divide_with_remainder(a: int, b: int) -> tuple:
    """Return quotient and remainder."""
    return a // b, a % b

# TODO: Call min_max and unpack the result
scores = [87, 92, 45, 78, 100, 63]
lowest, highest = min_max(scores)
print(f"Lowest: {lowest}, Highest: {highest}")

# TODO: Call divide_with_remainder and unpack
quotient, remainder = divide_with_remainder(23, 7)
print(f"23 / 7 = {quotient} remainder {remainder}")
hints: Return multiple values with: return value1, value2 (Python packs them as a tuple).

exercises.title: Early Return (Guard Clauses)
exercises.description: Use early returns to handle invalid inputs before the main logic. This avoids deeply nested code.
exercises.starterCode:
def safe_divide(numerator: float, denominator: float) -> float | None:
    """Divide two numbers; return None if denominator is zero."""
    if denominator == 0:
        return None   # Early return for invalid input
    return numerator / denominator

def grade_letter(score: float) -> str:
    """Return letter grade; return 'Invalid' if score out of 0-100 range."""
    if score < 0 or score > 100:
        return "Invalid"
    if score >= 90: return "A"
    if score >= 80: return "B"
    if score >= 70: return "C"
    if score >= 60: return "D"
    return "F"

# Test safe_divide
print(safe_divide(10, 2))
print(safe_divide(10, 0))

# Test grade_letter
print(grade_letter(95))
print(grade_letter(72))
print(grade_letter(105))  # invalid
hints: Return early when input is invalid — avoids nesting the main logic.
guard clauses are if checks at the start that return immediately.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: return Exits the Function
warmup.description: return both sends back a value AND exits the function immediately.
warmup.starterCode:
def absolute(n):
    if n < 0:
        return -n   # Exit here for negatives
    return n        # Exit here for non-negatives

print(absolute(-5))
print(absolute(3))
print(absolute(0))
